title: Elastic Potential Energy
summary: Energy in a squished spring. Trace it through a launch: spring → motion → height up a ramp.

steps.title: Energy stored in a squish
steps.body:
Pull back a bow, press down a trampoline, pull a pinball plunger — you're loading a spring with energy that springs right back.

A compressed or stretched spring stores **elastic potential energy**. Squeeze it more and it stores a lot more.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
**k** is the spring constant — how stiff it is (N/m). **x** is how far it's pushed or pulled from its natural length (m).

Because **x is squared**, the squeeze has more leverage than the stiffness: doubling **x** stores **4× the energy**, while doubling **k** only **doubles** it. (And since x is squared, pushing in or pulling out by the *same distance* stores the *same* energy.)

Load the spring below (push it in or pull it out) and change its stiffness to see it.

steps.prompt: A spring with stiffness **k = 200 N/m**. Drag the block to **squeeze** it and watch the stored energy — notice the x².
steps.task: Drag the squeeze to **double** it — from 0.3 m to 0.6 m. Then read the two bars.
steps.solution: Elastic PE is PE_s = ½kx², and the squeeze is squared. Compressing twice as far (0.3 m → 0.6 m) makes the x² term grow by 2² = 4, while the stiffness stays fixed. So the stored energy jumps from 9 J to **36 J — four times as much**.

steps.title: Let it go — the energy chain
steps.body:
Release the spring and the stored energy doesn't vanish — it flows. On a frictionless track it becomes **kinetic energy** as the block speeds off, and if the block then climbs, that motion converts into **gravitational PE**.

Follow one quantity of energy through the whole chain:

steps.title: Watch ½kx² worked through
steps.prompt: A spring with **k = 200 N/m** is compressed **0.40 m**. How much elastic PE is stored?
steps: Elastic PE is PE_s = ½kx² — and that x is **squared**, the step everyone rushes.
Now bring in the rest: PE_s = ½ × 200 × 0.16.
steps.takeaway: Square the stretch *before* multiplying — doubling the squish quadruples the stored energy.

steps.prompt: Your turn — setup's done. A spring with **k = 300 N/m** is compressed **0.40 m**. Finish the last line.

**Setup:** A spring with **k = 800 N/m** is compressed **0.25 m** and used to launch a **0.50 kg** block. The whole track is **frictionless**.

**Part A —** How much elastic PE is stored in the compressed spring?
hints: Square the compression first, then multiply by k and the ½.
steps.solution:
Elastic PE is PE_s = ½kx².

Square the compression: 0.25² = 0.0625. Then PE_s = ½ × 800 × 0.0625 = **25 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** The spring drives the **0.50 kg** block forward and releases it the instant the spring reaches its natural length (**x = 0**) — the point where *all* the stored energy has turned into motion. How fast is the block moving right then?
hints: At maximum compression v = 0 (all energy is PE_s); at x = 0 it's the reverse — all 25 J has become kinetic energy.
steps.solution:
Energy sloshes between PE_s and KE as the spring pushes. At full compression the block is momentarily at rest (all energy is elastic PE); by the time the spring reaches its natural length (x = 0) that PE has *fully* converted to kinetic energy, so the block is at its fastest here.

That's why we can write ½mv² = PE_s = 25 J.

v = √(2·PE_s / m) = √(2·25 / 0.50) = √100 = **10 m/s**.

steps.prompt: **Part C —** The block now coasts up a frictionless ramp. What maximum *vertical* height does it reach? (g = 9.8)
hints: At the highest point the block has stopped — all the energy is now gravitational PE.
Set mgh = 25 and solve for h: h = 25 / (0.50 × 9.8).
steps.solution:
At the top the block is momentarily at rest, so all the energy is gravitational PE: mgh = 25 J.

h = PE_s / (mg) = 25 / (0.50 × 9.8) = 25 / 4.9 ≈ **5.1 m**.

steps.prompt: **Part D —** If that ramp is tilted at **30°**, how far does the block travel *along the ramp's surface* before stopping?
hints: The 5.1 m is the vertical height; the ramp distance is longer. Use h = d·sin θ.
Rearrange: d = h / sin θ = 5.1 / sin 30° = 5.1 / 0.5.
steps.solution:
The height and the slope distance are linked by h = d·sin θ, so d = h / sin θ.

d = 5.1 / sin 30° = 5.1 / 0.5 ≈ **10.2 m** along the ramp.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You traced energy through a full chain — spring to motion to height — and tied it back to ramp geometry.
